description: The deposit and pay-into-firm command paths tell the player "insufficient personal funds" when the real cause is that the firm has no usable Treasury account. FirmTransactionServiceImpl.deposit()/payIn() call resolveAccountId() FIRST (before any funds check), which throws NoFirmAccountException for a firm whose default_account_id is unset or points at an archived/removed account. NoFirmAccountException extends IllegalStateException, so it is swallowed by the generic `catch (IllegalStateException)` and mapped to business.finance.insufficient-personal. The withdraw path (same file, lines 142-145) correctly catches NoFirmAccountException first and shows business.finance.no-account; deposit and payInto do not, so a proprietor with a broken/stale default account is told they are broke instead of that their firm has no account. This is exactly the class of 'error message that misstates the cause' the charter flags.
recommendation: In MiscCommands.doDeposit and MiscCommands.payInto, add a `catch (NoFirmAccountException e) { message.send(sender, "business.finance.no-account"); }` clause BEFORE the `catch (IllegalStateException)` clause, mirroring the withdraw handler at lines 142-145.
verification_note: Verified against all three cited files. NoFirmAccountException.java:13 confirms it extends IllegalStateException. FirmTransactionServiceImpl.deposit() (line 78) and payIntoFirm() (line 402) call resolveAccountId() before any funds check; resolveAccountId (line 283) throws NoFirmAccountException when the firm has no surviving account. In MiscCommands, doDeposit (lines 108-114) and payInto (lines 173-177) catch IllegalStateException -> business.finance.insufficient-personal with NO prior NoFirmAccountException catch, so the no-account condition is swallowed and misreported as 'broke'. The withdraw path (lines 142-145) and doSend (lines 333-334) DO catch NoFirmAccountException first -> business.finance.no-account, proving the inconsistency. Claimed snippet matches verbatim. Minor severity is fair: misleading error message on an edge case (unhealed default/stale/archived account with no survivor), no money-movement or crash impact.

description: disbandFirm/adminDisbandFirm run @Async and are not serialized against each other. Both read the firm, check `archived`, then call disbandInternal, which snapshots the account list and calls firms.archiveFirm(). archiveFirm's UPDATE (FirmMapper.archiveFirm) sets is_archived=1 with no `WHERE is_archived = 0` idempotency guard, so two concurrent disbands (e.g. a proprietor's `/firm disband confirm` racing an operator's `/firm admin disband`, or the public BusinessApi.disbandFirm racing a command) both pass the stale archived check and both snapshot the same positive per-account balance, then each issues a full-balance transfer to the proprietor. Currency is NOT duplicated because the ledger is authoritative: the second treasury.transfer draws from an already-drained account and Treasury's per-account overdraft floor (business accounts default allow_overdraft=false → floor 0) rejects it, and the exception is caught/logged per-account. So conservation holds, but safety here rests entirely on Treasury's floor rather than any guard in Business, and the losing thread logs a spurious drain failure.
recommendation: Guard the disband transition in Business: make FirmMapper.archiveFirm's UPDATE conditional (`WHERE firm_id = #{firmId} AND is_archived = 0`) and have disbandInternal short-circuit (return without draining) when archiveFirm reports 0 rows affected, so only the winning thread performs the drain. This removes reliance on Treasury's floor for conservation of the disband path.

description: Finance command handlers catch service-thrown exceptions and hand-format the error to a Message key, which the charter explicitly bans ("flag handlers that catch them to hand-format errors"). The handler infers business meaning from the JDK exception *type* (IllegalStateException => insufficient funds, SecurityException => no permission), a brittle coupling that also mis-labels: e.g. any IllegalStateException from deep in Treasury would be reported to the player as 'insufficient personal funds'. This repeats across all money-moving routes in MiscCommands (doDeposit 104-115, withdraw 136-151, payInto 167-178, payPlayer 200-212, payBusiness 246-260, doSend 324-340) and every finance route in AccountCommands (accountDeposit 368-380, accountWithdraw 402-414, accountPayInto 431-441, accountPayPlayer 464-477, accountPayBusiness 512-527).
recommendation: Once the service throws semantic exceptions (finding #1), remove these try/catch blocks entirely and let the framework surface the exception. If distinct Message keys per failure are required, attach the key to the semantic exception rather than switching on JDK exception type in the handler.

description: ParameterResolver.suggestions() runs off the main thread (the code's own comment says 'Suggestions run on a Netty thread'), yet it reads live Bukkit state via Bukkit.getOnlinePlayers(). The charter forbids resolvers touching live Bukkit state (Bukkit.getPlayer, world/entity access) from suggestions/resolve — the online-player set must come from a service-managed cache, not the server's live player list. Bukkit's online-player collection is not documented thread-safe for off-main iteration; List.copyOf mitigates a CME during copy but the read itself still crosses the async boundary into Bukkit internals. FirmSuggestionCache was explicitly designed (per its own Javadoc) to keep the Bukkit dependency out so the cache stays testable, but this resolver reintroduces it.
recommendation: Maintain the set of online-player UUIDs in a service (updated from PlayerJoin/PlayerQuit listeners on the main thread) and have the resolver union playerFirmNames over that cached UUID set, instead of calling Bukkit.getOnlinePlayers() on the suggestion thread.

description: FirmTransactionServiceImpl signals all user-facing business/validation failures (invalid amount, insufficient funds, missing authorization/access) with raw JDK exceptions — IllegalArgumentException, IllegalStateException, SecurityException — instead of the framework's semantic exceptions (BadCommandException, ExceedsLimitException, NoPermissionException). This is the sole money-moving service in the plugin doing so; every sibling service (FirmServiceImpl, FirmStaffServiceImpl, FirmRequestServiceImpl, FirmAccountServiceImpl, FirmRoleServiceImpl) throws BadCommandException/NoPermissionException/NotFoundException/ConflictException and lets them propagate. Because these exceptions are non-semantic, the framework cannot format them, forcing every finance command handler to wrap the call in try/catch and hand-map each JDK exception type to a Message key (see finding #2). The pattern repeats across every transfer method in the file (deposit lines 74-87, withdraw 136-160, depositToAccount 321-338, withdrawFromAccount 358-381, payIn 463-475, payOut 499-520, payFirm 439-441).
recommendation: Throw the framework semantic exceptions from the service: BadCommandException for non-positive/invalid amounts, ExceedsLimitException (or a domain 'insufficient funds' semantic exception) for insufficient personal/business funds, NoPermissionException for canAccessAccount/authorizer failures. Let them propagate so the framework renders them, and delete the try/catch blocks in the command handlers.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 74-87. FirmTransactionServiceImpl throws raw IllegalArgumentException/IllegalStateException/SecurityException for all validation/funds/access failures across every transfer method (deposit 74-87, withdraw 136-160, depositToAccount 321-338, withdrawFromAccount 358-381, payIn 463-475, payOut 499-520, payFirm 439-441). Confirmed sibling services (FirmServiceImpl, FirmRequestServiceImpl, etc.) throw framework semantic exceptions (BadCommandException/NoPermissionException/NotFoundException/ExceedsLimitException) which propagate and are framework-formatted. Confirmed the consequence: AccountCommands.java has ~14 hand-written catch blocks mapping these three JDK types to Message keys (lines 373-524), which the semantic-exception path would not require. Genuine convention divergence in the sole money-moving service; major is fair.

description: The taxInfo command handler performs economy arithmetic inline: it fetches the aggregate balance, looks up the weekly rate, and computes estimatedTax = totalBalance.multiply(rate).setScale(2, HALF_UP) plus a rate-to-percentage multiplication. The charter states economy arithmetic inside a CommandHandler is a finding. Worse, this exact computation (totalBalance.multiply(rate).setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)) duplicates the authoritative per-cycle logic in FirmBalanceTaxServiceImpl.buildFirmCollections (line 128), so the two can drift — the estimate shown to a player could diverge from what the tax cycle actually charges.
recommendation: Move the estimated-tax and rate-percentage computation into a service method (e.g. FirmBalanceTaxService.estimateWeeklyTax(firmId) returning an object with balance, rate, and tax), reusing the same rounding path as the collection logic, and have the handler only format and send the result.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at TaxCommands.java:84-91. taxInfo command handler performs economy arithmetic inline: totalBalance.multiply(rate).setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP) plus rate.multiply(100) percentage. Confirmed this exact expression duplicates FirmBalanceTaxServiceImpl.buildFirmCollections line 128 (BigDecimal totalTax = totalBalance.multiply(rate).setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)), so the displayed estimate and the actual cycle charge can drift. Charter flags handler-level economy arithmetic. Read-only display estimate, low blast radius; minor is appropriate.

description: RealtyRegionValidator.validate() calls Bukkit.getWorlds() (a Bukkit API call) and is reachable from an @Async command route: FirmCommands.setHq (@Async) -> FirmService.updateFirmHq -> FirmAreaShopService.isValidPlot -> RegionValidator.validate. The charter states no Bukkit API calls (world/entity access) from async contexts; results touching Bukkit state must hop back to the main thread. Bukkit.getWorlds() is a read that Paper generally tolerates off-thread, so this is not a crash, but it is a Bukkit-access-from-async-route violation of the stated rule and should be surfaced.
recommendation: Either snapshot the world list on the main thread and pass it in, or resolve HQ validation via the Realty service without iterating Bukkit.getWorlds() from the async path (e.g. accept a pre-resolved world/region identifier). At minimum document that this read is deliberately off-thread and confirmed safe on Paper.

description: ExpireRequestsJob injects and calls FirmRequestMapper directly (field 'requests' is a FirmRequestMapper, constructed at lines 20-24). The charter states services are the ONLY layer that may call mappers; a job is not a service. Every other consumer in the plugin reaches persistence through a service (FirmRequestService exists and is the natural owner of request expiry). This bypasses the service layer, so any validation/logging/transaction policy the service would enforce around expiry is skipped.
recommendation: Add an expireStale()/runExpiry() method to FirmRequestService (delegating to the mapper), inject FirmRequestService into ExpireRequestsJob instead of FirmRequestMapper, and call the service from run().

description: The charter calls for an interface + impl split for every service. FirmChatService is a concrete class bound as a singleton with no service interface (unlike FirmService/FirmServiceImpl, FirmStaffService/FirmStaffServiceImpl, etc.). It also is not wired through BusinessModule as an interface->impl binding; it is injected as the concrete type. FirmSuggestionCache is similarly interfaceless. These are in-memory state holders rather than DB-backed domain services, which is why the split was skipped, so the impact is low, but it is an inconsistency with the stated convention.
recommendation: If these are treated as services (they are injected into commands/resolvers), extract a FirmChatService interface with a FirmChatServiceImpl and bind it in BusinessModule for consistency; otherwise document them as intentional non-service helpers.

description: The firm-finance access predicate (proprietor OR ADMIN OR FINANCIAL) is copy-pasted verbatim across three command classes: AccountCommands.canAccessFirmFinances, MiscCommands.canAccessFirmFinances, and SalesCommands.canViewSales. This is the authorization gate for reading balances and moving firm money, so the three copies must stay in lock-step; a change to the predicate in one place silently diverges the others, which is exactly the kind of drift that produces an authorization bug on one command family but not another.
recommendation: Extract a single shared predicate (e.g. a FirmStaffService.canAccessFinances(firmId, uuid) method, since FirmStaffService already owns hasPermission and is injected everywhere) and have all three command classes call it. The service layer is the correct home for an authorization rule, not each command.
verification_note: Snippet at AccountCommands.java:586-590 matches verbatim. Confirmed identical predicate (isProprietor OR RolePermission.ADMIN OR RolePermission.FINANCIAL) at MiscCommands.java:394-398 and SalesCommands.java:254-259 (canViewSales). Three verbatim copies of the firm-finance authorization gate that must stay in lock-step; a change to one silently diverges the others. Minor severity is fair: it is a real DRY/auth-drift maintainability concern, not a current bug (all three copies are presently identical).

description: The transaction-history rendering loop (sign/formatted/time/msg build + header + next-page footer) is duplicated near-verbatim between MiscCommands.transactions and AccountCommands.accountTransactions, differing only in the i18n message keys. The two supporting constants TX_PAGE_SIZE = 10 and the identical TIME_FMT DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("MM/dd HH:mm") are also duplicated across both classes.
recommendation: Hoist the per-entry formatting (sign + amount + time + message) and the TX_PAGE_SIZE / TIME_FMT constants into a shared helper (a small formatter utility or a base command class), parameterised by the i18n key prefix, so both transaction views render through one code path.

description: The same domain identity (a firm) is addressed by three different parameter types across the service surface: String firmName (which is actually a name-or-id string, parsed downstream by getFirmByNameOrId), int firmId, and Integer firmId. This inconsistency forces lossy round-trips: the public API delegates in BusinessApiImpl repeatedly do String.valueOf(firmId) to feed String-typed services, and FirmStaffServiceImpl.hireEmployee/hireEmployeeFromInvite take Integer firmId but immediately call firms.getFirmByNameOrId(firmId.toString()) — turning a known id back into a string to re-parse it. Several ADT-99/ADT-108 comments elsewhere in the codebase already flag this int→String→int churn as a smell and add typed by-id paths, but the service interfaces still expose the String form widely.
recommendation: Standardise firm-addressing on int firmId in the service and API layer (matching the model's Integer firmId primary key), keeping String firmName only where a genuine name/id ambiguity from user input must be resolved (the command entrypoints). Remove the firmId.toString()/String.valueOf(firmId) round-trips once callers pass the id directly.

description: The Discord invite-URL validation regex "https://discord\\.gg/[A-Za-z0-9]{2,32}" is hard-coded in three separate places: FirmServiceImpl.updateFirmDiscord and both FirmCommands.setDiscord and FirmCommands.adminSetDiscord. Comments at each site ("ADT discord-url-minimessage-injection") show this is a security-relevant validation guarding against MiniMessage/injection payloads reaching the public /firm info card and the explorer. Three independent copies of a security regex are a drift hazard: tightening the pattern in the service (the last line of defence) won't automatically update the command-layer copies, and vice versa.
recommendation: Move the pattern into a single validator (e.g. NameValidator.isValidDiscordUrl(String) alongside the existing name validators, which already centralises anti-injection whitelists) and call it from all three sites. Keep the authoritative check in the service and let the commands reuse the same method.

snippet: List<FirmRolePermission> perms = roles.listPermissionsByRole(firmId, Objects.requireNonNull(roleName, "roleName must not be null").trim());
description: Objects.requireNonNull(roleName, ...) is dead defensive code: the immediately preceding line 252 already returns false when roleName is null or blank, so roleName is provably non-null (and non-blank) by the time line 254 runs. The requireNonNull can never trip and only obscures the .trim() call.
recommendation: Drop the Objects.requireNonNull wrapper and call roleName.trim() directly; the guard on line 252 already establishes the invariant.

snippet: injector.getInstance(CommandManager.class).registerAll();
description: No startup-shaped test builds the Guice injector (HiberniaModule + BusinessModule + DatabaseModule) and calls registerAll() on CommandManager/ListenerManager (Business.java lines 121-140). A grep of the entire test tree for Guice.createInjector / registerAll / BusinessModule / HiberniaModule returns nothing. The charter requires such a test so that command route conflicts, unbound ParameterResolvers, and missing/mistyped message keys fail in CI rather than only at server enable. Because message keys are referenced as bare string literals (e.g. message.send(sender, "business.firm.create.success", ...)), a typo'd or removed key currently has no compile-time or test-time guard — it only surfaces when a player runs the command in production.
recommendation: Add a wiring/smoke test that constructs the injector with the real modules (Treasury APIs mocked) and invokes CommandManager.registerAll() + ListenerManager.registerAll(), asserting no exception. If the framework exposes the registered command/route set and the loaded Message bundle, additionally assert every message key referenced by command handlers resolves against messages.properties, so route conflicts and missing keys break the build.

resolution.note: Shared test-kit + real injector-registerAll + bidirectional key-audit tests; green-gated

snippet: public class FirmChatService {
description: FirmChatService has zero tests (no test file references it anywhere under business/src/test). It owns real, privacy/moderation-critical logic that is trivially unit-testable without Bukkit statics: toggleGlobalSpy / toggleFirmSpy / isSpyingOn / isAnySpy (chat-spy semantics — global vs per-firm, add/remove toggle, empty-set cleanup at lines 145-178), the recipients() routing that dedups a spy who is also a tuned-in member and prevents cross-firm chat bleed (lines 109-143), forget() session cleanup (line 218), inMultipleFirms (line 225) and firmName (line 181). These methods depend only on the injected FirmService / FirmStaffService and a Business (for its logger) — all mockable. A regression here silently leaks firm-chat across firms or lets a de-toggled spy keep hearing, with no test to catch it.
recommendation: Add a FirmChatServiceImplTest (plain Mockito) covering: toggle round-trips (add then remove returns false / clears the map entry), isSpyingOn honouring globalSpies over firmSpies and null firmId, isAnySpy, forget() clearing all three maps, and recipients() excluding members whose active firm differs and de-duplicating a member who is also a spy. Mock FirmStaffService.getOnlineEmployees to return mocked Players.
verification_note: Snippet 'public class FirmChatService {' matches verbatim at line 37. Confirmed zero tests: grep -rl FirmChatService over business/src/test returns nothing, and the module has a *Test for nearly every other service (30+ test files), so this is a genuine gap in an otherwise thoroughly-tested, 0.95-line-gated module. The spy-semantics logic is real and non-trivial: toggleFirmSpy (154-164) has empty-set cleanup, recipients() (109-131) dedups a spy who is also a tuned-in member via a UUID-keyed LinkedHashMap and gates each firm's membership on activeFirm to prevent cross-firm bleed. Most cited methods (toggleGlobalSpy/toggleFirmSpy/isSpyingOn/isAnySpy at 145-178, forget at 218, inMultipleFirms at 225, firmName at 181) truly depend only on injected FirmService/FirmStaffService plus in-memory sets and are Bukkit-free/mockable, so the testability claim mostly holds. However the finding overstates it: recipients() and its helper addOnline (134-142) call Bukkit.getPlayer (a Bukkit static) and iterate staff.getOnlineEmployees returning Bukkit Players, so that method is NOT 'trivially unit-testable without Bukkit statics.' More importantly this is a pure test-absence finding: no actual defect in the code is demonstrated, only that it is untested. Per the repo's audit standard test-depth is somewhat deferred pre-prod, so a coverage gap with no shown bug does not warrant 'major'; it is a legitimate but minor coverage gap.

description: The three custom ParameterResolvers have no tests (no test file references FirmPlayerResolver, FirmNameResolver, or OnlineFirmNameResolver). These are pure/injectable and directly testable, and FirmPlayerResolver.resolve carries non-trivial branching the charter singles out: blank->empty, UUID-exact path, compact 32-char UUID re-hyphenation in tryParseUuid (lines 76-92), exact-name match, and prefix search that only resolves when unambiguous (matches.size()==1) — the ambiguity/no-match edge returns empty. None of these paths, nor the suggestions() distinct/last-known-name mapping, are exercised. FirmNameResolver/OnlineFirmNameResolver.suggestions (player vs console fallback, online-player snapshot union) are likewise untested.
recommendation: Add resolver unit tests with a mocked FirmPlayerService/FirmSuggestionCache: assert compact-32-char UUID resolves via findByUuid, an ambiguous 2-match prefix returns empty while a unique prefix resolves, exact-name takes precedence over prefix, and suggestions() dedups current names. For the name resolvers, assert player senders suggest from playerFirmNames and console falls back to activeFirmNames.

description: The test named withDriftFoldedIntoLargest does NOT exercise the drift-fold-into-largest branch it claims to cover. It uses equal balances 100/100/100 at rate 0.01, so each account's proportional tax is exactly 1.00 and the drift = totalTax - allocated is zero — the branch `if (drift.signum() != 0 && largestAccountId != null) perAccountTax.merge(largestAccountId, drift, ...)` in FirmBalanceTaxServiceImpl (buildFirmCollections) is never taken. The test's own comment admits 'no drift here'. This is the exact rounding/boundary behaviour the regression-lock charter wants pinned (per-account HALF_UP setScale(2) leaving a remainder that must be folded so the per-account sum equals totalTax); it is currently unverified, so a regression that drops or misapplies the drift correction (currency created/destroyed by a rounding cent) would not be caught.
recommendation: Add a case with unequal or non-evenly-divisible balances that produces a real remainder — e.g. balances 100/100/100 taxed at a total that doesn't divide by 3 (or two accounts 10.00 / 20.00 at a rate whose per-account HALF_UP amounts don't sum to totalTax) — and assert (a) the per-account amounts sum EXACTLY to totalTax, and (b) the drift cent lands on the largest-balance account specifically.

description: refundOnRemoval credits the shop owner FIRST (economy.deposit, which is a SYSTEM→owner transfer, a mint) and only afterwards debits the server-economy account (debitServerEconomy → economy.withdraw). The two legs are separate, non-atomic Treasury transfers. deposit() is void and swallows all exceptions (EconomyServiceImpl:110-125 only logs), and debitServerEconomy ignores withdraw()'s boolean result. There is no pre-check that the server-economy account can cover the refund. If the server-economy withdraw fails (insufficient funds / Treasury error) the owner has already been credited, so the refund is effectively minted from SYSTEM with no offsetting debit — the fee pool and the credited amount diverge and the 'refund comes from the collected-fee pool' invariant is broken. The player is also told the shop was refunded regardless of whether the mirror leg succeeded.
recommendation: Reverse the order and gate on the mirror leg: first attempt debitServerEconomy (checking withdraw()'s boolean) and only economy.deposit() the owner if it succeeds (or if no server-economy account is configured, treating SYSTEM as the deliberate faucet). Make deposit() return a success signal so the credit can be confirmed, and only send SHOP_REFUNDED after both legs commit.
verification_note: Snippet matches ShopServiceImpl.java:605-608 verbatim. refundOnRemoval credits the owner first via economy.deposit (SYSTEM->owner mint; deposit() is void and swallows exceptions at EconomyServiceImpl.java:110-125) and only afterwards calls debitServerEconomy (serverAccount->SYSTEM via economy.withdraw at 620-624, whose boolean result is discarded, and which is a silent no-op when getServerEconomyAccount() is null). There is no pre-check that the server-economy account can cover the refund, and no rollback of the owner credit if the mirror debit fails. This is the exact inverse of the correctly-ordered chargeCreationFee (569-586), which withdraws from the player first, checks the boolean, and returns on failure before crediting the server economy. So on any server-economy withdraw failure the owner keeps a refund minted from SYSTEM with no offsetting debit, breaking the fee-pool-conservation invariant, and the player is told SHOP_REFUNDED unconditionally. The defect and every sub-claim hold. Severity: major is fair — a real conservation break with wrong leg ordering, though bounded to the refund price on a rare failure path in a recoverable game economy.

resolution.note: chestshop atomic money legs + overflow clamp; test-first, green-gated

description: getAmountOfAffordableItems does walletMoney.divide(pricePerItem, 0, FLOOR).intValueExact(). When the balance/price quotient exceeds Integer.MAX_VALUE, intValueExact() throws ArithmeticException, which would propagate out of the partial-fill validation on the main thread and abort the trade with an unhandled exception rather than a clean outcome. This is reachable for the SELL branch: adjustSell feeds economy.getBalance(owner) as walletMoney, and getBalance returns BigDecimal.valueOf(Double.MAX_VALUE) whenever normaliseAdminTarget resolves to null (EconomyServiceImpl:164-168). For a cheap unit price (pricePerItem = 1) and that sentinel balance, the quotient overflows int. The isOwnerEconomicallyActive guard only short-circuits when there is NO server-economy account; a mis-scaled sentinel is still plausible under partial-fill config, and even a legitimately very rich owner selling a 1-priced item overflows.
recommendation: Clamp the quotient before converting, e.g. use intValue() with a Math.min against a sane max (the shop's max amount / stack count) or catch the overflow and treat it as 'more than enough' by capping at the requested item count. Never call intValueExact() on an unbounded currency÷price quotient in a main-thread trade path.

resolution.note: chestshop atomic money legs + overflow clamp; test-first, green-gated

description: chargeCreationFee correctly aborts if the player-side withdraw fails, but the mirrored creditServerEconomy(price) leg (SYSTEM→server-economy transfer) is unchecked: creditServerEconomy calls economy.deposit(), which is void and swallows exceptions (EconomyServiceImpl:110-125). If the deposit to the server-economy account fails, the player has already been charged the fee but the server-economy account is never credited — the money is left in the SYSTEM sink instead of the fee pool, silently drifting the two balances. The player is told SHOP_FEE_PAID regardless.
recommendation: Give deposit() a success return and check the creditServerEconomy leg; on failure either compensate (refund the player) or log a reconciliation warning rather than reporting SHOP_FEE_PAID unconditionally. At minimum surface the mirror-leg failure instead of dropping it in a debug log.

description: In the SHOP_DEPOSIT_FAILED branch the owner is notified with the message key 'chestshop.CLIENT_DEPOSIT_FAILED' (the client-side failure text), not a shop/owner-appropriate key. The owner receives a message that misstates whose deposit failed. The client separately gets the correct SHOP_DEPOSIT_FAILED key. This is a misleading error message — the owner is told the *client's* deposit failed when the failure is on the shop/owner side.
recommendation: Send the owner an owner-oriented key (e.g. a dedicated 'your shop could not receive payment' message) rather than reusing chestshop.CLIENT_DEPOSIT_FAILED, so the notification names the correct side of the failed deposit.

description: Listener registered on BlockPhysicsEvent (one of the charter's named hot events, fired for essentially every block-update in loaded chunks). The guard is not O(1)/allocation-free: handlePhysicsBreak(block) immediately calls BlockUtil.isSign(block) which invokes block.getBlockData() (SignBreakListener.java:64-66 -> BlockUtil.java:41-48), cloning a BlockData object on every physics tick before the cheap sign test can short-circuit. Charter: verify the guard prefix is O(1) and allocation-free; registration on hot events for rarely-relevant logic is a finding even if guarded. It also runs at MONITOR yet mutates state (deregisters the shop via sendShopDestroyed).
recommendation: Guard on the cheap Material check first (event.getBlock().getType() against Tag.SIGNS / a wall-sign set) before any getBlockData()/getState() call, so the vast majority of physics events return in O(1) with no allocation; only then fall through to the sign-break handling.

description: The @Route handler captures the whole tail as one greedy String, re-splits it into a token array, and hand-parses positional args: iterating indices, calling Bukkit.getPlayer(args[index]) and Integer.parseInt(args[index]) with its own index-bookkeeping (disregardedIndexes). The charter explicitly flags manual arg parsing (args[i], Integer.parseInt) where @Arg typing or a ParameterResolver should carry it, and requires @Route methods to hold only light normalisation + a service call. Player resolution and quantity parsing are argument-typing concerns that belong to typed @Arg / a player ParameterResolver, not the handler body.
recommendation: Model the signature with typed args (an optional player @Arg backed by a player ParameterResolver, an optional int quantity @Arg, and a greedy item-code arg) so the framework resolves player/quantity/code, and move the give logic (item parse + inventory add) into a service method the handler simply calls.
verification_note: Snippet matches GiveCommand.java:43-59 verbatim. The handler genuinely captures the tail as a greedy String, re-splits on \s+, and hand-parses positional args via Bukkit.getPlayer(args[index]) + Integer.parseInt(args[index]) with disregardedIndexes bookkeeping (lines 47-79). This is exactly the manual arg-parsing anti-pattern the charter flags, and GiveCommand is the only ChestShop command doing it (others use typed @Arg). However this is a convention/altitude violation only: the command works correctly, there is no correctness, money-movement, or security defect. 'Major' implies a functional/correctness problem; a working handler that violates the 'light normalisation + service call' altitude rule is a maintainability concern, so minor is the fair severity.

description: Command handler hand-formats an error to the sender with a hardcoded §-coloured string instead of a Message key, and catches the semantic failure to format it inline. Charter flags raw sender.sendMessage with hardcoded/legacy-colour strings in handlers.
recommendation: Send the error via a Message key (e.g. message.send(sender, "chestshop.iteminfo_error")); keep the log.error.

description: Command handler emits a hardcoded, legacy-§-coloured error string via raw sender.sendMessage instead of a Message key. Charter: all player-facing text via Message keys; flag hardcoded strings, legacy §/ChatColor codes, raw sender.sendMessage in handlers.
recommendation: Route through the already-injected Message bean with a key (e.g. message.send(sender, "chestshop.PLAYER_ONLY")).

description: Command handler emits player-facing text via raw sender.sendMessage(...) with hardcoded English strings and legacy section-code colours (Colours.* = §-codes) instead of Message keys. The charter requires all player-facing text through Message keys and flags raw sendMessage and legacy §/ChatColor codes in handlers. Other ChestShop commands correctly use the injected Message bean; VersionCommand does not even inject one.
recommendation: Inject Message and send both lines via Message keys (e.g. message.send(sender, "chestshop.version", "name", ..., "version", ...) and "chestshop.config_reloaded"), removing the hardcoded strings and Colours usage.

The same listener otherwise uses message.send(...) with keys, so this is inconsistent as well as a charter violation (hardcoded string, legacy colour, raw sendMessage).

description: SignService is a substantial injected @Singleton service (sign parsing, admin-shop/business detection, validity, item/owner/price/quantity extraction) but is a concrete class with no interface, unlike every other service in the component (AccountService/EconomyService/MarketService/... each split into interface + impl under services/impl/). Charter: interface + impl split for every service. It is injected across many listeners and services, so the omission is an architectural inconsistency, not just an internal helper.
recommendation: Extract a SignService interface and rename the class to SignServiceImpl (bind interface->impl in ChestShopModule), matching the plugin's consistent service split.

description: ChestShop#registerEvent is a public manual-listener-registration helper with no callers anywhere in the component (all listeners are registered via HiberniaModule.listeners(...) + ListenerManager#registerAll; the only manual registerEvents is the WorldEdit adapter, which uses its own plugin.getServer()...registerEvents). It is dead code that also advertises the framework-bypass registration pattern the plugin has otherwise moved off of.
recommendation: Delete the unused registerEvent method so the only sanctioned registration path is ListenerManager.

description: Four classes in listeners/ (entrypoint layer) hold business-layer logic that is invoked directly by the service layer, and are injected into services as collaborators — inverting the stated one-directional layering (entrypoint -> service -> persistence; services must not depend on entrypoints). MarketListener.onTransaction/onShopCreated/onShopDestroyed (market-DB sync) are called by TransactionServiceImpl/ShopServiceImpl, not by Bukkit; only onInventoryClose is a real @EventHandler. StockCounterListener.onTransaction/onPreShopCreation/updateCounterOnQuantityLine, RestrictedSignListener.onPreTransaction, and SignBreakListener.sendShopDestroyed are likewise service methods living in listener classes and injected into TransactionServiceImpl/ShopServiceImpl. The javadoc documents this as a leftover of collapsing the old event bus, but structurally the business logic belongs in services/ with the listeners as thin adapters.
recommendation: Move the service-invoked methods into proper @Singleton services (e.g. MarketSyncService, StockCounterService, RestrictedSignService, SignBreakService) that the pipeline services inject; keep only the genuine @EventHandler methods (onInventoryClose, onSignBreak, onSignChange, etc.) in the listener classes and have those thin listeners delegate to the new services. Services then depend on services, not on listeners/.

description: TransactionServiceImpl is a god class: 719 lines and 17 injected constructor dependencies (well above the ~10 charter threshold). It owns prepare/validate/execute/process end-to-end plus notification-cooldown state, error-message routing, empty-shop deletion, trade logging and message dispatch. The charter flags god classes at >~400 lines or >~10 deps and this exceeds both. Some sub-responsibilities were already peeled off (PartialFillCalculator, GoodsTransfer), but the remaining validation, messaging, post-trade reactions and empty-shop deletion are still all here.
recommendation: Extract the cohesive sub-responsibilities into their own collaborators: the validation pipeline (rejectInvalidClientName/rejectCreativeMode/flagFreeShop/checkFundsAndStock/checkPermissions/checkStockFits) into a TradeValidator; the error/trade messaging (sendErrorMessage/sendShopLocationMessage/sendTradeMessage/notificationCooldowns) into a TransactionMessenger; and deleteEmptyShop into the shop lifecycle service. This drops the dependency count below the threshold and gives each unit a single reason to change.

Constructor at lines 120-140 injects 17 dependencies (economy, shops, accounts, signBreak, stockCounter, message, items, market, config, signService, shopBlockService, inventoryService, adminBypass, restrictedSign, metrics, partialFill, goodsTransfer). Snippet matches verbatim. Class genuinely owns prepare (148), validate (250), execute (557), process (537), notification-cooldown state (notificationCooldowns table line 92, clearNotificationCooldowns 453), error-message routing (sendErrorMessage 460), empty-shop deletion (deleteEmptyShop 597), trade logging (logTransaction 655) and message dispatch (sendTradeMessage/sendMessageToOwner). PartialFillCalculator/GoodsTransfer already peeled off as claimed. Both charter thresholds (~400 lines, ~10 deps) exceeded; major is fair.

description: EconomyService.canHold(UUID, BigDecimal) unconditionally returns true and ignores both parameters, yet it is used as a real guard at two decision points in PartialFillCalculator (lines 118 and 184: `if (!economy.canHold(...))`). Those guarded branches are therefore permanently dead, and the method is a no-op masquerading as a balance-ceiling check — a maintenance trap (a reader assumes a capacity check happens where none does).
recommendation: Either implement a genuine ceiling check (Treasury has no per-account cap in this economy, in which case say so) or remove canHold from EconomyService and delete the two dead `if (!economy.canHold(...))` guards in PartialFillCalculator so the code states what it actually does.

description: Several static fields that are assigned once and never reassigned are declared as mutable `static` rather than `static final` constants — mutable static state the charter calls out. StringUtil.characters / StringUtil.extraWidth (the Minecraft char-width lookup tables) and Base62.ALPHABET / Base62.BASE are effectively constants but writable. Any code (including reflection or a future edit) can mutate shared global state, and the int[] table is additionally a mutable array reference.
recommendation: Declare these `private static final` (and for the int[] width table, treat it as immutable / consider an unmodifiable copy accessor). No behavioural change; removes the mutable-static footgun.

description: The vendored 1822-line Base64 implementation (iharder.net / Robert Harder) duplicates functionality the JDK has provided as java.util.Base64 since Java 8 (the module targets Java 21). Its full GZIP/encodeObject/file-IO surface is unused: the only remaining caller is ItemCodeServiceImpl.decodeBlob at line 327 (Base64.decode(blob)), a narrow fallback for line-broken legacy blobs that java.util.Base64's strict decoder rejects. Everything else in the file is dead weight and a maintenance/attack surface.
recommendation: Since the legacy migration (migrateBlobEncoding, PAR-290) rewrites rows to plain java.util.Base64, retire the vendored class once migration is confirmed complete, or at minimum trim it to the single MIME-decoding path decodeBlob needs (java.util.Base64.getMimeDecoder() handles line-broken input and would remove the whole vendored file).

description: The location-placeholder block (world/x/y/z with the ADT-140 null-world guard, plus price) is built twice with the same shape inside TransactionServiceImpl — once in sendShopLocationMessage (503-513) and once in sendTradeMessage (699-706). The two copies risk drifting (e.g. the null-world guard fixed in one but not the other).
recommendation: Extract a small private helper that populates the world/x/y/z (and price) placeholders from a Location into the replacement map/array, and call it from both message builders.
verification_note: sendShopLocationMessage (503-513) builds price + world/x/y/z placeholders with the ADT-140 null-world guard (loc.getWorld() != null ? loc.getWorld().getName() : "?"). sendTradeMessage (699-706) builds the identical shape with the same guard on line 702. Cited snippet matches lines 503-513 verbatim. The two copies duplicate the same location-placeholder block and can drift; nit is appropriate for a DRY observation.

description: No startup-shaped test exists that builds the Guice module (ChestShopModule) and calls CommandManager.registerAll()/ListenerManager.registerAll(). Grep across chestshop/src/test/** for Guice.createInjector, new ChestShopModule, CommandManager, ListenerManager, and registerAll returns zero hits. registerAll() is invoked only in production ChestShop.java. There are 11 annotation-driven @Command/@Route command classes; without a wiring smoke test, a duplicate route, a missing Guice binding, or a message key referenced by a command handler but absent from messages.properties will not fail in CI — it will surface only at server boot. The charter explicitly requires verifying such a startup test exists. MockBukkit is already available and used by 26 tests, so a headless boot test is feasible.
recommendation: Add a startup test that constructs the injector with ChestShopModule (over a MockBukkit server), resolves CommandManager and ListenerManager, and calls registerAll() on each, asserting no exception and no duplicate-route/missing-binding error. This pins route conflicts, missing bindings, and message-key gaps into CI.

resolution.note: Shared test-kit + real injector-registerAll + bidirectional key-audit tests; green-gated

description: The two custom MyBatis TypeHandlers (UuidStringTypeHandler, DateLongTypeHandler) and the two mappers (AccountMapper with 10 SQL statements, ItemCodeMapper with 8) are entirely untested. All service tests substitute a hand-written FakeAccountMapper / fake mapper, so the real @Select/@Insert SQL strings and the TypeHandler conversions are never exercised. The mappers/** and typehandlers are also excluded from the jacoco coverage gate (build.gradle.kts jacocoExcludes includes "io/paradaux/chestshop/mappers/**"), so the 0.99/0.99 gate provides no signal here. The TypeHandlers are pure (they take java.sql.PreparedStatement/ResultSet interfaces, no Bukkit statics) and the null-handling round-trip is directly unit-testable; the mapper SQL runs against a local SQLite file, which can be spun up in-process in a test (no Testcontainers/H2 dialect-mismatch concern since production is SQLite). A malformed SQL string or a TypeHandler regression would ship undetected.
recommendation: Add direct unit tests for UuidStringTypeHandler and DateLongTypeHandler (mock ResultSet/PreparedStatement, assert the string/long round-trip and null handling). Add an integration test that opens an in-process SQLite database, applies the mappers' createTable DDL, and round-trips AccountMapper/ItemCodeMapper CRUD so the annotation SQL is validated against the real dialect.

description: The notification-cooldown regression test asserts the owner is re-notified after the cooldown lapses by sleeping 1100ms of wall-clock time against a 1-second config window. This is a real-time-dependent concurrency/timing test — it adds >1.1s to the suite and is inherently flaky under CI load (a stalled JVM between the two validate() calls could push either side of the boundary). The root cause is in the SUT: TransactionServiceImpl.sendError (chestshop/src/main/java/io/paradaux/chestshop/services/impl/TransactionServiceImpl.java:523,526) reads System.currentTimeMillis() directly with no injectable Clock/time source, so the cooldown boundary cannot be exercised deterministically. This is the exact Thread.sleep-based-timing anti-pattern the charter flags.
recommendation: Introduce an injectable time source (e.g. a LongSupplier or java.time.Clock constructor-injected into TransactionServiceImpl) and replace the two System.currentTimeMillis() reads at TransactionServiceImpl.java:523 and :526 with it. Then drive the cooldown boundary in the test by advancing the fake clock instead of Thread.sleep(1100), removing the wall-clock dependency and the 1.1s runtime cost.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at TransactionServiceImplTest.java:2016-2018. The test sendError_ownerNotification_resendsAfterCooldownExpires does Thread.sleep(1100) against a 1-second cooldown (config.getNotificationMessageCooldown()=1L) and asserts sendMessage is called twice. Root cause confirmed: TransactionServiceImpl.sendMessageToOwner reads System.currentTimeMillis() directly at lines 524 and 527 (the finding's 523/526 are off by one but point at the correct block) with no injectable Clock/time source, so notificationCooldowns (a HashBasedTable of raw millis) cannot be advanced deterministically. The test is genuinely real-time-dependent, adds >1.1s to the suite, and is flaky (only a 100ms margin over the 1s window, so a GC/scheduler stall between the two validate() calls can cross the boundary). All factual claims hold. Severity downgraded to minor: this is an isolated test-quality/flakiness defect on a single test method with a bounded blast radius (an occasional CI flake and ~1s cost), not a production correctness bug and not a repeated pattern; major overstates the impact.

description: When a bracket entry's key parses as a valid numeric floor but its rate VALUE is unparseable (e.g. an empty string, or a typo like "5%"), the NumberFormatException from `new BigDecimal(e.getValue())` is caught by the single catch block whose message reads "Invalid balance tax bracket key '<key>'". The cause is actually the value, not the key, so the operator's warning misstates which field is wrong and points them at a floor that is in fact valid — confusing behaviour that can send debugging in the wrong direction. Additionally a `null` value is silently coerced to rate 0 with no warning at all (line 63), so a blank/missing rate silently becomes a 0% bracket rather than being flagged like out-of-range and unparseable entries are.
recommendation: Split the parse: catch the key parse and value parse separately (or inspect which token failed) so the warning names the offending field, e.g. "Invalid balance tax bracket rate '<value>' for floor '<key>'". Treat a null/empty value the same as an unparseable one (warn and skip) rather than silently coercing to 0, so a mistyped rate is surfaced rather than becoming a silent 0% bracket.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 62-72 of BalanceTaxBrackets.java. Both defects hold: (1) the key parse (line 62) and value parse (line 63) share one try block, and the sole catch (lines 70-72) always emits "Invalid balance tax bracket key '<key>'" — so an unparseable RATE value (e.g. "5%", empty string) throws from line 63's new BigDecimal but is reported as a bad key, misdirecting the operator to a floor that is actually valid. (2) Line 63's `e.getValue() == null ? "0"` silently coerces a null/missing rate to 0.0, which passes the [0,1] range check on line 64 and is added as a valid 0% bracket with no warning, unlike out-of-range/unparseable entries which are flagged. Severity minor is fair: this is operator-facing config-diagnostic quality (misleading warning text plus a silent default), not a ledger/money-movement correctness bug.

description: The public factory `fromRawEntries` treats `rawEntries` as nullable (explicit `!= null` guard at line 58) but has no null contract for `onWarn`: if a caller passes `null` and any entry is invalid (out-of-range rate at line 65 or unparseable key at line 71), `onWarn.accept(...)` throws NullPointerException. The parameter is not annotated `@Nullable`/`@NonNull` and the javadoc describes what `onWarn` receives without stating it is required. This is an inconsistent null contract between the two parameters of the same method on a shared-kernel public API.
recommendation: Either fail fast at the top of the method with `Objects.requireNonNull(onWarn, "onWarn")` to make the required contract explicit and give a clear message, or document `onWarn` as `@NonNull` in the javadoc. Prefer `requireNonNull` for symmetry with the defensive `rawEntries` handling.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 56-72. rawEntries is explicitly null-guarded (line 58) while onWarn is dereferenced unconditionally at lines 65 and 71 with no @Nullable/@NonNull annotation and no javadoc null contract, so a null onWarn on an invalid-entry path NPEs. The asymmetric null contract on a shared-kernel public factory is real. nit severity is fair: the defect only surfaces on the invalid-entry branch and any reasonable caller supplies a real consumer.

description: DataSourceProvider has no test class at all (common/src/test has only BalanceTaxBrackets, JwtKeys, OverdraftPolicy, UuidBin). This is the config-drift-prevention class ADT-184 created to consolidate three divergent Hikari factories, yet none of its load-bearing logic is exercised: the statementCaching URL branch (lines 39-43 appending useServerPrepStmts/cachePrepStmts/rewriteBatchedStatements), the invariant JDBC URL and charset (lines 35-38), the always-on autoCommit=false and MariaDB driver (lines 52-53), the five conditional overrides (connectionTimeout/maxLifetime/keepalive/leakDetection/transactionIsolation at lines 56-64 each gated on a sentinel), and the Builder defaults (lines 84-92). The class is testable in a plain unit test: build.gradle.kts already puts HikariCP on testImplementation (line 23), HikariDataSource extends HikariConfig so getJdbcUrl()/isAutoCommit()/getTransactionIsolation() are assertable after build() (use a Testcontainers MariaDB, or set a short initializationFailTimeout, to avoid a live-DB connect). A regression that, say, dropped the utf8 charset param, flipped autoCommit, or broke the statementCaching branch would ship undetected.
recommendation: Add DataSourceProviderTest asserting: default URL contains the mariadb/host/port/db and useUnicode=true&characterEncoding=utf8; statementCaching(true) appends the server-prep-stmt/cache/rewriteBatchedStatements params and statementCaching(false) does not; autoCommit is false and driver is org.mariadb.jdbc.Driver; each conditional override (connectionTimeout/maxLifetime/keepalive/leakDetection/transactionIsolation) is applied when set and left at Hikari default when the sentinel is unchanged; Builder defaults (poolName, poolSize 10, minIdle 2). Drive build() against a Testcontainers MariaDB (production dialect, not H2) so the URL and pool actually connect.
verification_note: Snippet matches lines 26-30 verbatim, and the absence of a DataSourceProviderTest is confirmed (common/src/test has only BalanceTaxBrackets, JwtKeys, OverdraftPolicy, UuidBin). The described logic all exists: statementCaching URL branch (39-43), invariant URL/charset (35-38), autoCommit=false + MariaDB driver (52-53), the five conditional overrides (56-64), Builder defaults (84-92). testImplementation(hikaricp) is present (line 25, not 23 as claimed, but the point holds). However, the class is pure framework-free config plumbing: no arithmetic, no ledger interaction, no money movement, no balance writes. Its only 'logic' is JDBC URL string-building and gated setters. A regression here (dropped charset, flipped autoCommit, wrong driver) fails fast at pool init on first boot rather than silently corrupting data. The finding also understates test friction: build() eagerly constructs new HikariDataSource(cfg), which attempts a live connection with no initializationFailTimeout seam, so 'plain unit test' actually requires Testcontainers or timeout tricks. Per the audit standard (test depth deferred pre-prod, ADT-1 ruler), a missing test on a config-only factory whose regressions surface at boot is a minor quality gap, not major.

description: JwtKeysTest does not cover the null/blank rejection branch (lines 47-49). This is input validation on the single cross-trust-boundary derivation used by both mint (treasury-api-plugin) and verify (treasury-rest-api); a missing/empty secret must fail loudly rather than derive an all-empty-passphrase key. No test asserts deriveHmacKey(null) or deriveHmacKey("")/"   " throws IllegalStateException.
recommendation: Add tests asserting deriveHmacKey(null), deriveHmacKey(""), and deriveHmacKey("   ") each throw IllegalStateException with the "JWT secret must be set" contract.

description: UuidBinTest (only roundTripsAnyUuid and nullSafe) has no test for the ADT-147 wrong-length guard. This guard is an explicit, commented regression fix (lines 26-31: a non-16-byte buffer previously threw an opaque BufferUnderflowException when too short or silently truncated when too long, yielding a wrong-but-plausible UUID). A behaviour with obvious historical-bug shape has no pinning test, so a refactor that dropped the length check would reintroduce the silent-corruption bug without any test failing.
recommendation: Add tests asserting UuidBin.fromBytes(new byte[15]) and fromBytes(new byte[17]) each throw IllegalArgumentException (not BufferUnderflowException and not a truncated UUID), pinning the ADT-147 fix.

description: The legacy passphrase path (JwtKeys lines 67-70: SecretKeySpec of MessageDigest SHA-256(secret UTF-8)) is only exercised indirectly — the tests assert it is deterministic, 32 bytes, and HmacSHA256, but nothing pins the derivation to the exact SHA-256 digest bytes. Because mint and verify (different JVMs, different datacenters) must derive byte-identical keys or every token silently fails to verify, a change to the legacy KDF that remained deterministic and 32-byte (e.g. hashing base64 of the secret, or adding a salt/prefix) would pass all existing tests while breaking the trust boundary for every deployment still on a passphrase secret. There is no regression lock pinning the legacy output to a known vector.
recommendation: Add a test that pins the legacy derivation to a fixed vector: compute MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256").digest(secret.getBytes(UTF_8)) for a constant passphrase and assertArrayEquals it against deriveHmacKey(passphrase).getEncoded(), so any drift in the passphrase KDF fails CI.

description: Money stored as DECIMAL(19,2) is summed for display KPIs by re-parsing each string to a JS number (IEEE-754 double) and adding in JS, instead of summing in SQL as the rest of the DAL does. Individual DECIMAL(19,2) values up to ~9e15 are exactly representable, but accumulating many of them in a double introduces representation/rounding error, so the displayed rollup can drift from the exact ledger figure by fractions of a cent up to cents once enough rows contribute. This is a website/API-consistency issue (charter item 8): the viewer's totals can disagree with the ledger's exact sums and, within a single view, with SQL-computed siblings. It is display-only — the ledger is never written and per-row values are shown exactly — hence minor, not a currency-integrity defect. The pattern repeats across the whole viewer; every KPI/rollup that sums money uses parseFloat+float addition rather than a DECIMAL sum.
recommendation: Sum money in SQL (as done for businessWealth/getTotalSupply/getMoneyFlow) and pass the string total to fmtAmtFull, or sum the minor-unit integers (multiply cents) in JS and divide only at format time. At minimum make each view's windowed total use the same SQL-exact path as its all-time sibling so the two figures can't disagree. Never fold DECIMAL money through parseFloat before summing.

Severity minor is fair — a website/ledger-consistency nit bounded to sub-cent/cent rounding on rollups, not a currency-integrity defect; the finding explicitly self-limits to that.

description: The searchParams-flattening helper (Record<string,string|string[]|undefined> -> Record<string,string>) is copy-pasted into 18 page modules under the names `flat` and `flatten`. The copies have drifted into three behaviourally distinct variants: (a) the guarded form shown here (`v.length > 0 && v[0] !== undefined`), (b) a truthy form `if (v[0]) out[k] = v[0]` (app/me/market/page.tsx:325, app/chestshop/items/[itemKey]/page.tsx:307) which drops legitimately-empty-string array params, and (c) a strings-only form `if (typeof v === 'string') out[k] = v` (app/admin/accounts/page.tsx:78) which silently discards array-valued params entirely. Divergent behaviour across copies of the same helper is exactly the DRY hazard: a query-string edge case behaves differently depending on which page received it.
recommendation: Extract one shared helper (e.g. `flattenSearchParams` in lib/, colocated with the zod SP parsing) with a single agreed semantics for array-valued and empty-string params, and replace all 18 local definitions with an import. Pick the guarded variant (a) as canonical.

description: Two `as never` casts on a write path suppress Kysely's Insertable/Updateable column-name and type checking for the explorer_identity insert/upsert. The explorer_identity table is fully typed in the DB registry (lib/db.ts:41-47) and uuidToBin returns Buffer matching player_uuid_bin, so the cast is not required by an untyped registry entry — it blanket-erases the value-object type, meaning a future typo in a column name or a wrong-typed value on this economy-identity write would compile silently rather than being caught. This is the `as`-cast-hiding-type-errors anti-pattern the charter calls out, on a mutation path.
recommendation: Remove the `as never` casts and let the value objects type-check against the registry. If Kysely rejects them because a generated/default column (linked_at) is declared required, mark that column via Kysely's Generated<> in the DB interface (or ColumnType) rather than casting the whole payload to never.

Stub node:dns lookup to test the resolution path.

description: The integration suite (test/integration/db.ts loads test/integration/schema.sql) runs the real lib/sql queries against a real MariaDB — good, no H2 dialect mismatch. But the DDL it runs is a 364-line hand-maintained snapshot, NOT the authoritative economy-flyway migrations. The schema-drift CI gate (economy-explorer-schema-drift.yml) only regenerates and diffs lib/db.generated.ts against a Flyway-migrated DB; it never touches test/integration/schema.sql (grep confirms no reference). So a Flyway column rename/drop/added-NOT-NULL can leave schema.sql stale while the integration tests keep passing green against the old shape — the very drift class ADT-5 set out to prevent, but for the integration fixture rather than the TS types. The tests then assert behaviour that no longer matches production DDL.
recommendation: Either run the integration tests against a Flyway-migrated database (apply economy-flyway migrations to the CI MariaDB service, drop the checked-in schema.sql), or add a drift check that diffs a mysqldump of the Flyway-migrated schema against test/integration/schema.sql and fails on divergence. The first is preferable — it removes the snapshot entirely.

resolution.decision: Run Flyway in test harness vs add CI drift check — pick one strategy (also treasury-rest-api/testing/0009).

description: requireRole is the tier gate for /me, /admin/*, /government — a permission-edge behaviour the charter calls out for pinning tests. It is pure (only depends on the Viewer type and error classes) and trivially unit-testable, yet untested (the only 'requireRole' reference in test/ is a comment). Its RANK comparison with the `viewer.role === 'player' ? 'player' : viewer.role` normalisation is a boundary-prone expression (a viewer whose role is 'player' vs a legacy/unknown role) that has no regression lock. requireAdmin (lib/auth/requireAdmin.ts) has the same status: pure, gates every admin server action, no test.
recommendation: Add a unit test matrix: anon throws UnauthorizedError; player meets 'player' but is rejected for 'government'/'admin' with ForbiddenError; government meets 'government' but not 'admin'; admin meets all three. Do the same for requireAdmin (admin passes; player/government/anon throw ForbiddenError).

description: resolvePlayerUuid contains the only non-trivial pure logic in the group service — trimming, UUID-shape detection, and lower-casing a canonical UUID before falling through to a name lookup. It gates admin-typed member identifiers into the RBAC group. It has no unit test (grep 'resolvePlayerUuid|services/group' across test/ finds nothing). The UUID-vs-name branch and the toLowerCase normalisation are testable without a DB by injecting/stubbing findPlayerUuidByName, but the module reaches the DAL directly so it needs a light mock; the trim+regex+lowercase path can be asserted directly.
recommendation: Add a unit test for resolvePlayerUuid: a mixed-case canonical UUID returns lower-cased verbatim without hitting the DAL; a non-UUID string is trimmed and delegated to findPlayerUuidByName (stub it); whitespace is stripped. setGroupCapabilities's transactional replace is better left to an integration test.

description: isDiscordWebhookUrl is pure (explicitly documented as having no server-only deps) and mirrors the dispatcher's DiscordWebhook.java host/path allowlist — a classic drift-prone mirror pair. It has no test (grep 'discord|isDiscordWebhookUrl' across test/ finds only an unrelated schema column). The host set (discord.com, discordapp.com, canary/ptb subdomains) and the path regex /^\/api(\/v\d+)?\/webhooks\/\d+\/.+/ are untested, so a typo or a divergence from the Java side would not be caught.

description: CI runs `npm run coverage` (vitest run --coverage) on every PR, but the coverage block declares no thresholds (grep for 'threshold|thresholds|lines|branches' in vitest.config.mts returns nothing). Coverage is therefore reported but never enforced — the run cannot fail on a coverage regression, unlike the JVM modules which have jacoco line/branch gates wired into `check` (treasury/business 0.95, chestshop 0.99). New untested code (e.g. the ssrf/discord/requireRole gaps above) can land without any signal. The `coverage` script and CI step give a false impression of a gate that does not exist.
recommendation: Add a coverage.thresholds block (e.g. lines/functions at the current measured baseline) so the CI coverage step fails on regression and ratchets upward, matching the repo's stated coverage-gate convention.

snippet:
@Select("SELECT lp.posting_id, lp.txn_id, lp.amount, lp.memo, " +
            "       lt.message, lt.settlement_time, lt.initiator_uuid_bin, lt.plugin_system " +
            "FROM ledger_postings lp " +
            "JOIN ledger_txns lt ON lp.txn_id = lt.txn_id " +
            "WHERE lp.account_id = #{accountId} " +
            "ORDER BY lt.settlement_time DESC, lp.posting_id DESC " +
            "LIMIT #{limit} OFFSET #{offset}")
description: The REST API's paginated account transaction history filters on ledger_postings.account_id but orders by the *joined* ledger_txns.settlement_time DESC. No index supports this: ledger_postings has idx_postings_account(account_id), idx_postings_txn(txn_id), and idx_postings_account_txn_amount(account_id,txn_id,amount) — none can provide settlement_time ordering, which lives in the joined table. Every page therefore materialises all of the account's postings, joins to ledger_txns, and filesorts, degrading as an account's history grows. The in-process Treasury LedgerMapper.findTransactionsByAccount deliberately orders by p.posting_id DESC (served index-only by idx_postings_account, with an explicit comment 'Do not revert to ORDER BY t.settlement_time') — so the two engines return reverse-chronological history via different mechanisms and the REST one is the slow, unindexed variant.
recommendation: Align the REST query with the Treasury one: ORDER BY lp.posting_id DESC (posting_id is auto-increment, monotonic with insert time, and covered by idx_postings_account so the sort is a backward index range scan with no filesort). This also removes the behavioural divergence between the two engines' history ordering.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at LedgerMapper.java:60-66 — the REST query filters WHERE lp.account_id and ORDER BY lt.settlement_time DESC, lp.posting_id DESC. Index claim verified in V1__initial.sql:143-145: ledger_postings has exactly idx_postings_txn(txn_id), idx_postings_account(account_id), idx_postings_account_txn_amount(account_id,txn_id,amount); no later migration adds a posting index. None carries settlement_time (that column lives on ledger_txns), so the ORDER BY forces a full materialization of the account's postings, a join to ledger_txns, and a filesort — degrading with history size. The contrast is real: treasury/.../mappers/LedgerMapper.java:112-113,180-181 deliberately uses ORDER BY p.posting_id DESC with the exact comment 'Do not revert to ORDER BY t.settlement_time', served index-only by idx_postings_account. Both engines return reverse-chronological history but the REST one is the slow, unindexed variant. Severity minor is fair — a performance/index-coverage divergence, not a correctness or money-loss bug.

snippet:
@Select("SELECT d.delivery_id AS deliveryId, d.subscription_id AS subscriptionId, d.txn_id AS txnId, "
            ...
            + "WHERE d.status = 'PENDING' AND d.next_attempt_at <= NOW() "
            + "ORDER BY d.delivery_id ASC LIMIT #{limit}")
description: The webhook dispatcher's due-poll filters WHERE status='PENDING' AND next_attempt_at <= NOW() but orders by delivery_id ASC. The supporting index is idx_delivery_due(status, next_attempt_at) (V13) — it seeks the due set efficiently but does not carry delivery_id, so the ORDER BY delivery_id requires a filesort of the matched (PENDING+due) rows on every poll tick. The set is normally small (a retry outbox), so impact is bounded, but under a delivery-failure backlog the PENDING set grows and every poll re-sorts it.
recommendation: Either order by (next_attempt_at, delivery_id) so the existing idx_delivery_due leading columns satisfy the sort, or extend the index to idx_delivery_due(status, next_attempt_at, delivery_id) to make the current delivery_id ordering index-served. Confirm the dispatcher's fairness requirement (oldest-enqueued vs earliest-due) before picking.

snippet: "next-auth": "^5.0.0-beta.25",
description: Production authentication for the economy-explorer is pinned to a pre-release beta of next-auth (declared ^5.0.0-beta.25, resolved 5.0.0-beta.31 in package-lock.json). next-auth v5 has been in beta for an extended period; betas make no API-stability or security-patch guarantees, and a `^` range on a `-beta.N` version resolves forward across breaking beta increments (beta.25 -> beta.31 already happened). This is the auth layer of a money-adjacent read surface, so churn or an unpatched auth regression here has outsized impact.
recommendation: Pin next-auth to an exact beta version (drop the caret: "5.0.0-beta.31") so beta bumps are deliberate, and track the v5 GA release to migrate off the beta line as soon as it ships. Do not upgrade blindly across betas without reading the changelog for auth-flow breaking changes.
verification_note: Snippet "\"next-auth\": \"^5.0.0-beta.25\"," matches verbatim at economy-explorer/package.json line 27. package-lock.json confirms it resolved forward to 5.0.0-beta.31, so the beta.25->beta.31 forward-resolution claim is accurate. next-auth is genuinely the auth layer (lib/auth/authjs.ts, middleware.ts, types/next-auth.d.ts). Depending on a floating pre-release for the auth dependency is a legitimate dependency-hygiene concern. Note the 'outsized impact' framing is mild overstatement: this is a read-only explorer over a recoverable game economy, and npm treats ^5.0.0-beta.25 as >=5.0.0-beta.25 <5.0.0 (it will not jump to 5.0.0 stable), limiting drift to beta increments. Still a fair minor as a hygiene item on an auth dep, not a downgrade to nit given it is the auth layer.

snippet: hibernia = "1.2.0-SNAPSHOT"
description: The Hibernia Framework — the shared Paper foundation bundled (shaded) into every deployable plugin jar (treasury, business, treasury-api-plugin, chestshop) — is pinned in the version catalog to a moving `-SNAPSHOT`. A SNAPSHOT coordinate resolves to whatever the latest published snapshot is at build time, so two builds of the same monorepo commit can shade different framework bytecode into the release/ plugin jars. For a foundational library that is compiled into shipped artifacts this defeats build reproducibility and makes it impossible to tie a released plugin jar to an exact framework revision. (paper-api's `1.21.11-R0.1-SNAPSHOT` is not comparable — Paper only publishes the API under the -SNAPSHOT coordinate, so it is a fixed name, not a floating snapshot the way an io.paradaux snapshot is.)
recommendation: Cut a fixed hibernia-framework release (e.g. 1.2.0) and pin the catalog to it before any release build, so a given monorepo commit shades a deterministic framework version. If snapshot iteration is needed during development, keep the snapshot only on develop and require a fixed version for the develop->main release PR.

resolution.decision: Cut hibernia-framework 1.2.0 release and pin — external (framework repo).

snippet:
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=60s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -sf http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1
description: The image runs with SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod (Dockerfile line 26). The prod profile relocates the actuator management server to a separate port: application-prod.yaml lines 69-70 set `management.server.port: ${MANAGEMENT_PORT:8081}`, while `server.port` is 8080 (line 26). Actuator endpoints (health/info/prometheus) are therefore served on 8081, NOT 8080. The container HEALTHCHECK probes http://localhost:8080/actuator/health, which does not exist on the app port — the health probe will always return a non-2xx/connection result and the container will be perpetually reported unhealthy. The UAT profile (application-uat.yaml lines 64-65) relocates it identically, so any profile actually used in the image is broken.
recommendation: Point the HEALTHCHECK at the management port: `curl -sf http://localhost:8081/actuator/health` (or 127.0.0.1:${MANAGEMENT_PORT:-8081}). Keep it aligned with the management.server.port used by the active profile, and EXPOSE 8081 as well.
verification_note: Snippet matches Dockerfile:31-32 exactly. Defect holds: Dockerfile:26 sets SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod; application-prod.yaml:69-70 relocate the actuator to management.server.port ${MANAGEMENT_PORT:8081} while server.port is 8080 (line 26), so /actuator/health is NOT served on 8080. The HEALTHCHECK CMD curl http://localhost:8080/actuator/health therefore fails and the container is reported perpetually unhealthy. UAT profile (application-uat.yaml:64-65) relocates identically. Claim is technically accurate. Downgraded from major to minor because the stated production deploy path is Argo CD/k8s (workflow comments confirm kustomize overlays + Argo CD Image Updater), and Kubernetes ignores the Docker image HEALTHCHECK directive entirely in favor of its own liveness/readiness probes — so the broken directive has no effect on the actual deployment and only bites plain-docker/compose/local runs. Real misconfig, muted blast radius.

description: The Harbor push workflows use raw `docker build`/`docker push` rather than docker/build-push-action, and the image build/push steps are not pinned to action SHAs — but more concretely, the workflows push a mutable tag (`prod`/`latest`) alongside the immutable `<env>-sha-<short>` tag on every build. The immutable sha tag is good; however there is no provenance/SBOM attestation and no `--provenance`/`--sbom` emission, so the pushed money-service image carries no supply-chain metadata for the immutable tag Argo CD deploys. Combined with no scan (finding #4), a compromised or vulnerable layer has no detection surface.
recommendation: Switch to docker/build-push-action@<pinned-sha> with `provenance: true` and `sbom: true`, or add a buildx step emitting an SBOM attestation, so each immutable sha tag has verifiable provenance. Keep the sha tag as the deploy target (already the case).

description: The per-run salary dedup key buckets wall-clock time by the configured interval (currentPeriodStart = now - now mod interval), while SalaryTask.schedule fires a Bukkit timer every intervalSeconds ticks. The two clocks are independent: the timer's actual fire cadence can drift relative to the interval-bucket boundary (server lag, tick skew, restart re-schedule). If two consecutive scheduled fires happen to fall inside the same interval bucket, the second run's transfers all collapse onto the first run's dedup keys via the ledger uq_ledger_dedup constraint, so that whole period's salaries are silently skipped (paid++ never runs — the transfer returns the existing txn id). Conversely a bucket can be skipped. This is the documented ADT-8 tradeoff (favor never-double-pay over never-miss), so it is an intentional, bounded behaviour rather than a defect, but under sustained tick lag a payout period can be dropped with no operator-visible signal beyond the info-level 'Paid N salaries' line.
recommendation: Either derive the salary run id from the same monotonic scheduler tick/sequence that drives SalaryTask (so bucketing and firing share one clock), or key the dedup on an explicit incrementing run counter incremented once per SalaryTask.run() rather than on wall-clock/interval arithmetic. If the current behaviour is accepted, add a debug/info log when payout() collapses a payment onto an existing txn (transfer returned a pre-existing id) so a persistently-dropped period is diagnosable.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim: SalaryServiceImpl.java line 126 `long runId = currentPeriodStart();`, lines 128-132 the loop and `byte[] dedupKey = Idempotency.sha256("salary:" + runId + ":" + payment.player());`. The mechanism described is real: currentPeriodStart() at lines 159-163 buckets wall-clock via `now - Math.floorMod(now, interval)`, while SalaryTask.schedule (SalaryTask.java:42-45) fires a Bukkit timer at `intervalSeconds * 20L` ticks. These are genuinely independent clocks (wall-clock bucket vs tick-counted timer), so under sustained tick lag a fire could land in an already-paid bucket (period silently skipped via the ledger dedup UNIQUE) or a bucket could be missed. HOWEVER the code comment at lines 121-125 explicitly documents this as the intentional PAR/ADT-8 tradeoff (favor never-double-pay over never-miss), and the finding text itself concedes this is 'an intentional, bounded behaviour rather than a defect.' A self-admitted, documented, intentional design choice whose only residual concern is weak operator-visible signaling is an observability nit, not a minor defect. Downgrade to nit.

resolution.decision: Salary dedup clock — low-confidence, downgraded; decide fix vs formally reject.

description: The membership/authorizer-based spend authorization for GOVERNMENT accounts lives entirely in the command layer (GovCommand.canTransferFrom / hasAuthorizerAccess / isAllowed and FineCommand.canFineFrom), not in the service. The charter says services must re-validate and enforce authorisation and not trust handlers. LedgerServiceImpl.transferInternal enforces only the account-level requires_authorization gate; a caller that reaches the service directly (or a future second caller) can move money out of any government account without the member/authorizer check, because that check is not part of the service contract. The single existing caller happens to guard correctly, so this is a layering/duplication risk rather than a live exploit.
recommendation: Move the per-account member/authorizer spend check into the service layer (e.g. a GovService/LedgerService method that takes the acting UUID and throws NoPermissionException), and have the handlers delegate to it, keeping the command layer to messaging only. This also removes the duplicated canTransferFrom/canFineFrom logic across GovCommand and FineCommand.

description: The charter states resolver suggestions(...) runs off the main thread and must never touch live Bukkit state (Bukkit.getPlayer, world/entity access), being backed by service-managed caches instead. PayTargetResolver.suggestions iterates Bukkit.getOnlinePlayers() directly — live Bukkit state read from the suggestion thread on every keystroke. The government-name half of the suggestion is correctly served from a TTL cache; only the online-player enumeration reaches into live server state. Paper's online-player collection is a live mutable view, so an off-thread iteration can race concurrent join/quit mutation.
recommendation: Serve online-player suggestions from a service-managed cache updated on join/quit (or snapshot the roster on the main thread and hand the resolver an immutable copy), mirroring how governmentNames() is cached, so suggestions() touches no live Bukkit collection.

@EventHandler(priority = EventPriority.MONITOR, ignoreCancelled = true)
    public void onJoin(PlayerJoinEvent event) {
description: PlayerLoginListener handles PlayerJoinEvent at EventPriority.MONITOR while dispatching state-mutating work (recordLogin writes economy_players; balance tax collection writes the ledger). The charter flags MONITOR handlers that mutate state — MONITOR is conventionally reserved for pure observation of the final event outcome. Here the mutation is DB-side (not event/world state) and correctly hopped to an async task, so this is low-impact, but the priority choice is non-idiomatic for a handler whose purpose is to perform side-effecting persistence. FirstPlayerJoinEvent handles the same event at default priority, so the two join handlers also disagree on priority.
recommendation: Use the default (NORMAL) priority for a side-effecting join handler, reserving MONITOR for read-only observation; align both PlayerJoinEvent handlers on a deliberate, documented priority.
