description: There are two classes with the identical fully-qualified name io.paradaux.treasury.utils.Idempotency — one in the plugin (treasury/src/main) and one in the published treasury-api jar (treasury/treasury-api/src/main). The plugin depends on treasury-api, so both land on the classpath with the same package+name (a split-package duplicate). Their sha256 logic is identical, so behaviour happens to agree today, but this is a genuine DRY/structure defect: whichever copy the classloader resolves is non-deterministic, and the two can silently diverge. Contrast with utils/UuidBin.java, which was deliberately turned into a thin alias delegating to :common (ADT-22) instead of a copy — Idempotency should have received the same treatment (delegate to the api copy) rather than being duplicated verbatim.
recommendation: Delete the plugin's io.paradaux.treasury.utils.Idempotency and import the treasury-api io.paradaux.treasury.utils.Idempotency at the 5 call sites (GovServiceImpl, BalanceTaxService, LedgerServiceImpl, SalaryServiceImpl, GovDedupKeys), or make the plugin copy a thin delegating alias exactly as utils/UuidBin does for :common.
verification_note: Verbatim snippet matches treasury/src/main/.../utils/Idempotency.java lines 6-18 exactly. A second class with the identical FQN io.paradaux.treasury.utils.Idempotency exists in treasury-api/src/main, and the plugin declares implementation(project(":treasury:treasury-api")) at build.gradle.kts:33 — so both copies land on the runtime classpath as a split-package duplicate with non-deterministic classloader resolution. Their sha256 logic is behaviourally identical today (same SHA-256/UTF-8 digest) but can silently diverge. The UuidBin contrast is accurate: UuidBin.java was deliberately made a thin delegating alias to :common (ADT-22 documented in-file), the exact treatment Idempotency should have received. Genuine DRY/structure defect; major is defensible because a divergence would corrupt ledger dedup-key derivation.

If the Skipped/Failed handling were deleted and both fell through to the success path, these tests would still pass as long as collectTax is invoked. recommendation: Assert the observable consequence of Skipped/Failed handling: that collect() returns/does not throw and performs no follow-up side effect that a successful collect would (e.g. verify no additional ledger/notifier interaction, or assert the method's return/counter), so deleting the branch fails the test.

It is a dead i18n entry — a maintenance smell that a startup key-coverage test (see finding 1) would surface bidirectionally. (The placeholder.* keys are framework-consumed and legitimately unreferenced in this module's code.)
recommendation: Either remove the unused treasury.general.player-only key, or wire the player-only guard that was presumably intended to use it; add a bidirectional key-coverage assertion to the startup test so defined-but-unused and used-but-undefined keys both fail CI.

description: Link-code redemption is a non-atomic SELECT-then-DELETE: findValidLinkSub (a plain SELECT gated only on expires_at > NOW()) and the later deleteLinkCode are separate statements with no row lock, no DELETE ... RETURNING, and no check of the delete's affected-row count. Two concurrent `/treasuryapi ui link <code>` invocations (both routes are @Async, so they run on separate command threads) can both pass the validity check before either deletes the row, so a code intended to be single-use can be redeemed twice. Because a code maps to one keycloak_sub, the second redeemer's upsertIdentity overwrites the first's identity row (ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE player_uuid_bin = VALUES(...)), so whoever wins the race owns the explorer identity for that Keycloak subject. A shared/leaked code is thus redeemable by an unintended player, silently rebinding the explorer login to a different Minecraft account.
recommendation: Make redemption atomic: have deleteLinkCode return the affected-row count (or use a DELETE ... WHERE code=? AND expires_at>NOW() that also yields the sub, e.g. via a conditional DELETE guarded then SELECT, or a single UPDATE ... SET redeemed_by claim pattern) and only proceed with upsertIdentity when exactly one row was claimed by this caller. Reject (link.invalid) when the claim affects 0 rows so a concurrent/second redemption cannot rebind the identity.
verification_note: Snippet matches doLink verbatim at UiAccessHandler.java:111-133. ExplorerUiMapper confirms the two statements are non-atomic and unlockable: findValidLinkSub (line 31-32) is a plain SELECT gated only on expires_at > NOW() with no FOR UPDATE; deleteLinkCode (line 34-35) is a separate DELETE returning void, so no affected-row count is checked; upsertIdentity (line 37-41) uses ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE player_uuid_bin = VALUES(...), so a second redemption on the same keycloak_sub overwrites the identity binding. The ui link route is @Async (TreasuryAPICommand.java:161), so two concurrent invocations run on separate command threads and can both pass findValidLinkSub before either deletes the row, allowing a single-use code to be redeemed twice and rebinding the explorer login to a different Minecraft account. The described race and its consequence hold. Minor severity is fair: real identity-binding hijack but with tight preconditions (attacker must know a valid, unexpired, unredeemed code issued to a specific web user, and must win a narrow SELECT-before-DELETE window) and low blast radius (a game-economy explorer login, not the money ledger).

description: doUserRemove and doUserList resolve the target via resolveUuid, which calls Bukkit.getOfflinePlayer(name) with no hasPlayedBefore()/isOnline() guard — unlike doUserAdd (lines 79-83), which deliberately rejects never-seen names to avoid fabricating a deterministic offline UUID (ADT-38). For a mistyped name, removeRole matches 0 rows and the operator is told "{player} did not have the {role} role" and listRoles returns an empty list rendered as "player (no elevated roles)". Both outcomes are harmless (no wrong data written) but the message misstates the cause: the real issue is the name was never seen, yet the operator is told the (fabricated) account simply lacks the role. This is a confusing-behaviour inconsistency with the add path rather than a data defect.
recommendation: For consistency with doUserAdd, apply the same hasPlayedBefore()/isOnline() guard in doUserRemove and doUserList (or in resolveUuid) and emit treasuryapi.ui.user.unknown-player when the name was never seen, so the operator learns the name was unrecognised rather than being told a fabricated account lacks the role.

description: UiAccessHandler is a command-layer handler (delegated to from the @Route methods in TreasuryAPICommand for /treasuryapi ui ...) yet it injects ExplorerUiMapper directly and performs all persistence (role add/remove/list, link-code lookup/redemption, identity upsert) straight from the handler. There is no ExplorerUiService interface+impl. This violates the stated layering rule that services are the ONLY layer calling mappers and that a command handler must not inject a mapper. It also means input validation (VALID_ROLES check, role normalisation) and the link/keycloak orchestration live in the command layer rather than a re-validating service. The sibling key handlers (PersonalKeyHandler, BusinessKeyHandler) correctly route through ApiKeyService, making this an inconsistency as well as a violation.
recommendation: Introduce an ExplorerUiService (interface + impl) that owns ExplorerUiMapper and KeycloakAdminClient, performs role validation and the link/keycloak orchestration (throwing semantic exceptions on invalid role / unknown link code), and expose only that service to UiAccessHandler. Move mapper injection out of the handler.

verification_note: Snippet verified verbatim at the cited lines. UiAccessHandler (in commands/) is @Injected with ExplorerUiMapper (line 32/39-41) and calls it directly: addRole (86), removeRole (96), listRoles (106), findValidLinkSub (113), upsertIdentity (119), deleteLinkCode (120) — all raw persistence. ExplorerUiMapper is a genuine MyBatis @Mapper with INSERT/DELETE/SELECT. No ExplorerUiService exists in services/ (only ApiKeyService and KeycloakAdminClient). Input validation (VALID_ROLES, role .toLowerCase normalisation) and the link/Keycloak orchestration (lookup→upsert→delete→setMinecraftAttributes) all live in the handler. Sibling handler PersonalKeyHandler correctly injects ApiKeyService (never a mapper), confirming the inconsistency. This directly violates the CLAUDE.md rule 'A command must not inject a mapper' and 'persistence holds all DB access / command handler must not inject a mapper', which CLAUDE.md explicitly labels a defect. Severity major is fair — a genuine, in-module-inconsistent layering violation, not a correctness/money blocker.

description: UiAccessHandler injects the Guice Injector and calls injector.getInstance(GroupReconciliationTask.class) as a service locator from business/command code, which the DI-discipline charter explicitly flags. The lazy-resolve is used to avoid a hard binding when LuckPerms is absent, but this is exactly a service-locator antipattern; a Provider<GroupReconciliationTask> (or a guarded optional binding surfaced behind a small service) would express the optionality without handing the whole injector to a handler.
recommendation: Replace the injected Injector + injector.getInstance(...) with an injected com.google.inject.Provider<GroupReconciliationTask> (or move self-sync behind a service that owns the optional dependency). Remove the Injector field from the handler.

description: Authorisation for reissue/revoke is enforced entirely in the command-layer handlers (canManage / getOwnerUuid checks), not in the service. ApiKeyService.reissueKey/revokeKey take only a keyId and perform no ownership/proprietorship re-validation, so the service trusts the handler to have authorised. The charter requires services to re-validate inputs and enforce authorisation (don't trust handlers), throwing semantic exceptions. A second caller of ApiKeyService could rotate or revoke any key by id with no ownership check. Same gap in PersonalKeyHandler.doReissue/doRevoke.
recommendation: Push the ownership/proprietorship authorisation into ApiKeyService (pass the acting UUID, throw NoPermissionException/NotFoundException from the service), so the invariant holds regardless of caller; keep the handler's pre-check only for user-friendly messaging.

description: UiAccessHandler is a command-layer handler (package commands/) that injects a persistence mapper (ExplorerUiMapper) directly and owns real business logic: role validation against VALID_ROLES, player-existence checks, the link-code redemption flow (findValidLinkSub -> upsertIdentity -> deleteLinkCode) and Keycloak attribute orchestration with partial-failure handling. The stated layering (commands -> services(+impl) -> mappers; 'A command must not inject a mapper') is followed everywhere else in this plugin: PersonalKeyHandler and BusinessKeyHandler delegate to ApiKeyService, and no command handler in the sibling business/treasury plugins injects a mapper. This handler skips the service layer entirely, so its multi-step transactional logic (link redemption, role grant auditing) lives in the command tier with no service seam and is untestable without the command harness.
recommendation: Introduce an ExplorerUiService (interface + services/impl/ExplorerUiServiceImpl) that owns the mapper, the role-validation/link/keycloak orchestration and the @Transactional link-redemption sequence, mirroring ApiKeyService/ApiKeyServiceImpl. Reduce UiAccessHandler to thin delegation plus Bukkit-facing player resolution and Message sends, matching PersonalKeyHandler/BusinessKeyHandler.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 28-33. UiAccessHandler lives in commands/ yet injects the persistence mapper ExplorerUiMapper directly (imports line 7; field/ctor lines 32, 39-41) and owns business logic: VALID_ROLES role validation (line 30, 72-74), player-existence guard (79-83), and the multi-step link-redemption flow findValidLinkSub->upsertIdentity->deleteLinkCode (113-120) with Keycloak partial-failure handling (122-131). The convention is real and followed elsewhere: PersonalKeyHandler/BusinessKeyHandler delegate to ApiKeyService (verified PersonalKeyHandler injects ApiKeyService, not a mapper), and grep over treasury/commands and business/commands shows zero command handlers importing mappers. CLAUDE.md explicitly states 'A command must not inject a mapper'. The handler skips the service seam, leaving transactional link/role logic untestable without the command harness. Major is fair for a clear, consistently-violated layering rule (no runtime correctness bug, so not higher).

This is exactly the string-vs-enum pattern the repo convention flags (AccountType enum, not strings): a typo in any equals()/passed literal fails silently as a not-found rather than a compile error, and the valid-value set is documented only in a Javadoc comment.

@Singleton
public class KeycloakAdminClient {
description: Service-layer placement is inconsistent: ApiKeyService uses an interface in services/ with the concrete class in services/impl/ (ApiKeyServiceImpl), while KeycloakAdminClient is a concrete service class placed directly in services/ with no interface. Both are legitimate patterns individually, but within one small plugin the reader cannot infer from the package whether services/ holds interfaces or concretions.
recommendation: Pick one convention for this plugin: either give KeycloakAdminClient a services/ interface + services/impl/ concretion to match ApiKeyService, or accept concrete services in services/ and document it. Low priority — no behavioural impact.

resolution.decision: Interface+impl convention for ALL injected services (decide once; applies with business/pa/0007, chestshop/pa/0008).

description: BusinessKeyHandler is untested. Its canManage gate encodes an explicit audit fix (ADT-111): reissue/revoke of a firm-scoped BUSINESS key must be gated on CURRENT proprietorship of the key's firm, not the original issuer. Also untested: the firm-not-found and non-proprietor branches on doIssue, the type/null discrimination in doReissue/doRevoke, and the terminal-revoked pre-check (ADT-110). BusinessApi and ApiKeyService are injected interfaces; Player and Firm are mockable — the handler is fully unit-testable with fakes. A regression that reverted canManage to an issuer-identity check, or dropped the firmId!=null null-guard (NPE on a personal key mis-typed), would go undetected.
recommendation: Add a BusinessKeyHandlerTest with a fake BusinessApi (firms().getFirm/isProprietor) and fake ApiKeyService: assert issue rejects non-proprietors and unknown firms, reissue/revoke reject a non-current-proprietor, canManage returns false when firmId is null, and the revoked pre-check fires. Confirm the tests fail if canManage's isProprietor call is replaced by an issuer==sender check.

description: PersonalKeyHandler has zero tests, yet it contains security-relevant authorization logic: the owner-UUID equality gate on doReissue/doRevoke, the key-type discrimination (rejecting a BUSINESS key id passed to a personal command), the null-key not-found branch, and the terminal-revoked pre-check (ADT-110). Every collaborator (ApiKeyService, TreasuryApi, Message) is an injected interface and Player is trivially mockable/fakeable — the class is fully unit-testable in the same hand-rolled-fake style already used by ApiKeyServiceImplTest, so nothing structural blocks coverage. A regression that dropped the owner check (letting any player rotate/revoke another player's key) or inverted the type check would ship silently.
recommendation: Add a PersonalKeyHandlerTest with a fake ApiKeyService and a stub Message recorder: assert doReissue/doRevoke send the no-access key when the sender is not the owner, the not-found key when getKey returns null or a BUSINESS-typed key, the revoked key when the key is revoked, and that reissueKey/revokeKey are invoked only on the success path. Verify each test fails if the corresponding guard is deleted.
verification_note: Snippet matches PersonalKeyHandler.java:63-72 verbatim. doReissue/doRevoke both contain security-relevant gates: null/type check (line 65,88 rejecting non-PERSONAL keys) and the owner-UUID equality gate (line 69,92 key.getOwnerUuid().equals(sender.getUniqueId())), plus the terminal-revoked pre-check (line 76, ADT-110). No test in src/test references PersonalKeyHandler (grep -rl empty). All collaborators (ApiKeyService, TreasuryApi, Message) are injected interfaces and ApiKey is a Lombok @Data model with getKeyType/getOwnerUuid/isRevoked, so it is fully unit-testable in the same anonymous-fake style ApiKeyServiceImplTest already uses (new ApiKeyMapper(){...}). A regression dropping the owner check would let any player rotate/revoke another player's key and ship silently. Major is fair for an untested authorization gate on a security-sensitive command surface.

description: None of the four mappers (ApiKeyMapper, ExplorerGroupMapper, ExplorerUiMapper, UuidBinaryTypeHandler) has any integration test, while sibling plugins treasury and business run their mappers against a real MariaDB (MariadbContainerExtension) and treasury-rest-api uses EmbeddedMariaDb. The SQL invariants here are load-bearing and only asserted via in-memory fakes in ApiKeyServiceImplTest, never against a real dialect: the `AND revoked = 0` reissue guard that makes revocation terminal (ADT-110, and which must stay behaviour-equivalent to treasury-rest-api's rotateKey), the DISTINCT firm_employee JOIN with is_current=1 in findBusinessAccessibleByEmployee, the INSERT IGNORE / ON DUPLICATE KEY upserts, the BINARY(16) UUID round-trip via UuidBinaryTypeHandler, and the useGeneratedKeys keyId back-fill. A SQL typo or a schema drift in any of these would compile and pass the current suite.
recommendation: Add a mapper integration test using the same MariaDB harness the sibling plugins already use (MariadbContainerExtension / the economy-flyway schema). Cover at minimum: reissue returns 0 rows for a revoked key and 1 for an active key; findBusinessAccessibleByEmployee returns keys only for is_current=1 employment; the UuidBinaryTypeHandler round-trips a UUID. Do not substitute H2 — the production dialect is MariaDB and INSERT IGNORE / ON DUPLICATE KEY / BINARY semantics differ.

description: GroupReconciliationTask's only test covers the pure nodeKey() helper. The safety-critical mass-revoke guard in apply() — refuse to prune when the desired set is empty but current members exist (the documented 'never mass-revoke on a transient empty result' invariant) — has no pinning test, and neither does matcherFor() (the group-vs-permission node interpretation, a sibling of the tested nodeKey with the same branch structure). apply() is private and only reachable through run(), which calls the LuckPerms-bound resolveMembers(), so as written the guard is not unit-testable without a live LuckPerms; that packaging is itself the reason the highest-risk branch here is unverified.
recommendation: Extract the desired-vs-current diff-and-apply step (including the empty-desired guard) into a package-private method taking (int groupId, Set<UUID> desired) plus the injected ExplorerGroupMapper, and pin it: empty desired + non-empty current performs no removes; empty desired + empty current is a no-op; a normal diff adds/removes the expected UUIDs. Also add matcherFor() unit assertions mirroring the existing nodeKey tests (bare name and group.<x> -> InheritanceNode; dotted -> PermissionNode).

description: UiAccessHandler is untested and, unlike the other two handlers, is partly wired to hidden Bukkit statics (Bukkit.getOfflinePlayer in doUserAdd/resolveUuid, Bukkit.getPluginManager in doSelfSync). That static coupling is a framework-seam smell: the unknown-player guard (ADT-38, refusing to grant a privileged role to a fabricated offline UUID) and the invalid-role rejection cannot be exercised in a plain unit test without a Bukkit mock, so a regression in either security guard is untestable as structured. The role-validation set membership itself (VALID_ROLES) is pure and could be tested if the name->UUID resolution were behind an injected seam.
recommendation: Introduce a small injected PlayerResolver seam (name -> Optional<UUID>/played-before) so doUserAdd's unknown-player guard and doUserRemove/doUserList are unit-testable without Bukkit statics, then add a UiAccessHandlerTest covering: invalid role rejected before any DB write, unknown player rejected, valid grant writes the role and logs the audit line, remove reports granted vs not-granted from the mapper's affected-row count.

description: KeycloakAdminClient has no tests. Two pieces are testable without a live Keycloak: the per-subject lock-stripe selection lockFor (ADT-113, which must be deterministic per sub and always in-bounds — Math.floorMod guards the negative-hashCode case that a raw % would not) and the env-var-precedence secret selection in serviceAccountToken (TREASURYAPI_KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET overriding config, ADT-38). Neither is pinned, so a regression reintroducing a raw % (negative index -> ArrayIndexOutOfBounds for subjects with a negative hashCode) or dropping the env-var precedence would not be caught.
recommendation: Make lockFor package-private (or add a stripeIndex(String) helper) and assert it returns the same in-range index for the same sub and handles a subject string whose hashCode is negative. If the secret-selection logic is extracted to a package-private helper, pin the env-var-wins-over-config precedence.
verification_note: Snippet matches KeycloakAdminClient.java:52-54 verbatim. Math.floorMod(sub.hashCode(), LOCK_STRIPES) is the deterministic in-bounds lock-stripe selection (a raw % would yield a negative index for negative hashCodes -> ArrayIndexOutOfBounds), and serviceAccountToken (lines 109-112) implements the TREASURYAPI_KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET env-var-over-config precedence (ADT-38). No test references KeycloakAdminClient. Both behaviors are pure/testable without a live Keycloak. Minor is fair: it is a testing gap on non-authorization helper logic with small blast radius, not a security gate.

description: ApiKeyServiceImplTest is solid on the JWT mint and reissue-guard paths but leaves two service methods with no behavioural assertion: revokeKey (only ever wired as a fake returning 0 or 1, never asserted to delegate to mapper.revoke) and the two list delegators listKeys/listBusinessKeysAccessibleByEmployee (fakes return List.of(), and no test asserts the service passes through the owner/type arguments or the returned list). These are thin delegators, so the gap is low-risk, but they are testable behaviour with no lock. Also note the reissue tests assert only that an exception is thrown, not that no token was minted/returned on the 0-rows path — a mild under-assertion of the 'never mint an unusable token' intent.
recommendation: Add assertions that revokeKey invokes mapper.revoke with the given keyId, and that listKeys/listBusinessKeysAccessibleByEmployee return exactly the mapper's list and forward the ownerUuid/keyType arguments. Optionally strengthen the 0-rows reissue test to assert the exception message rather than only the type.

description: The admin transfer endpoint accepts an Idempotency-Key and TransferService.adminTransfer computes the same UNIQUE client_dedup_key (sha256Hex(fromAccountId + ':' + idempotencyKey)) as the token path, but the admin controller does NOT wrap the call in withIdempotencyReplay(...) the way TransferController does. TransferService.transfer's idempotency pre-check is check-then-insert, so two concurrent admin transfers with the same fromAccountId + Idempotency-Key both pass the findByDedupKey pre-check; the loser's insertTxn then violates the client_dedup_key UNIQUE constraint, throwing DuplicateKeyException. GlobalExceptionHandler has no handler for DuplicateKeyException, so it falls through to handleUnexpected and returns 500 INTERNAL_ERROR instead of replaying the winner's committed transfer. The identical race on the public /transfers path is handled by withIdempotencyReplay and returns the cached response.
recommendation: Wrap adminFirmService/adminTransfer invocation in the same withIdempotencyReplay(Supplier) retry-once pattern used by TransferController (extract it to a shared helper), or add a @ExceptionHandler(DuplicateKeyException) in GlobalExceptionHandler that re-runs/looks up and replays. Behaviour should match the token path: a same-key concurrent replay returns the original transfer, not a 500.
verification_note: Verified all four load-bearing claims by reading the cited files. AdminTransferController.java:44-45 calls transferService.adminTransfer(...) directly with no withIdempotencyReplay wrapper, unlike TransferController which wraps every public path (lines 72, 99, 128) in withIdempotencyReplay that catches DuplicateKeyException and retries. TransferService.adminTransfer (line 137) delegates to executeTransfer with the same idempotencyKey, and executeTransfer computes the identical dedup key sha256Hex(fromAccountId + ":" + idempotencyKey) at lines 183-184. The idempotency logic is genuinely check-then-insert: findByDedupKey pre-check at line 186, then insertTxn against the UNIQUE client_dedup_key constraint at line 264 — so two concurrent same-(fromAccountId,key) admin transfers can both pass the pre-check and the loser's insert throws DuplicateKeyException. GlobalExceptionHandler has no @ExceptionHandler for DuplicateKeyException; the fall-through handleUnexpected(Exception.class) at lines 102-108 returns 500 INTERNAL_ERROR. Severity minor is fair: it is a real but narrow concurrency race on the SERVICE-only, @Hidden admin endpoint; the winner's transfer still commits correctly (the UNIQUE constraint prevents any double-spend/ledger corruption), so the only impact is a wrong 500 error code instead of an idempotent replay on the losing concurrent retry — no money or data-integrity consequence.

description: disband() reads each firm account's balance via listFirmAccounts (a non-locking snapshot SELECT against account_balances_mat) and then sweeps that exact snapshot amount. executeTransfer re-acquires the balance under SELECT ... FOR UPDATE and re-checks the overdraft floor against the fresh value, so the sweep can never overdraw. But the swept amount is the stale snapshot value: under InnoDB REPEATABLE READ the FOR UPDATE read sees the latest committed balance while the swept 'balance' variable is the earlier snapshot. If a concurrent committed transfer credits the firm account after the snapshot read but before the sweep within the disband transaction, only the snapshot amount is moved to the proprietor and the residual delta is left in the account — which is then archived on the next line. Money is stranded in an archived account (not destroyed), so it is recoverable by an admin but no longer sweeps with the firm. The archived-firm idempotent guard does not help because the firm is now marked archived.
recommendation: Sweep the freshly-locked balance rather than the snapshot: within executeTransfer (or a dedicated sweep entry point) move min-of-full-locked-balance, i.e. read the FOR UPDATE balance and transfer that amount, rather than passing a pre-read snapshot amount. Alternatively acquire the FOR UPDATE lock on each firm account at the start of disband before reading the balance to sweep, so the read and the sweep amount agree.

description: The 1-based-page → int-offset conversion with overflow guard is duplicated verbatim between ChestShopService.validateOffset (lines 161-172) and TransactionService.getTransactions (lines 85-91). Both compute (long)(page-1)*limit, reject offsets > Integer.MAX_VALUE with the same 'page is too large' 400, and cast down to int. The totalPages Math.ceil computation (ChestShopService line 222, TransactionService line 100) is likewise duplicated. This is the pagination arithmetic the whole API depends on; a change to the cap or message in one place silently diverges from the other.
recommendation: Extract the offset-overflow computation and totalPages into one shared helper (e.g. a small Pagination utility in a common location for the module) and call it from both services so the bound and error contract live in one place.

occurrences.snippet: return (int) Math.ceil((double) totalItems / limit);
int totalPages = (int) Math.ceil((double) totalItems / limit);

description: The byte[] → lowercase-hex encoding loop using Character.forDigit((b >> 4) & 0xF, 16)/(b & 0xF) is copy-pasted between WebhookService.newSecret (line 133) and HmacSha256.hex (lines 27-31). TransferService.sha256Hex (lines 494-498) is a third hex encoder using a slightly different idiom (String.format("%02x")). Three hand-rolled hex encoders for the same operation invite drift and are individually re-implementing a stdlib-level utility.
recommendation: Consolidate to a single hex helper (e.g. java.util.HexFormat.of() from the JDK, which returns lowercase hex directly) and call it from newSecret, HmacSha256.hex, and TransferService.sha256Hex.

snippet: private static final DecimalFormat MONEY = new DecimalFormat("#,##0.00");
description: MONEY is a shared static java.text.DecimalFormat, which is documented as NOT thread-safe. It is used from money() (line 84) via toPayload() (line 78), which the webhook dispatcher invokes for Discord deliveries. WebhookDispatcherService.deliverDue() fans the batch across a bounded fixed thread pool of deliveryConcurrency (default 8) threads (init(), line 111-119; invokeAll, line 200), and each deliver() calls DiscordWebhook.toPayload for a Discord target (line 230). Concurrent format() calls on one DecimalFormat instance can interleave the internal DigitList/FieldPosition state, producing garbled amount strings (e.g. mixed-up digits) or an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException — corrupting the money value shown in a delivered Discord embed, or failing the delivery outright.
recommendation: Do not share a mutable DecimalFormat across threads. Either construct a fresh DecimalFormat inside money() per call, wrap it in a ThreadLocal<DecimalFormat>, or format with a thread-safe alternative (e.g. String.format("%,.2f", amount.abs()) or a java.util.Formatter local to the call). Since money() already builds a small string, a per-call formatter is simplest and cheap relative to the outbound HTTP POST.
verification_note: Snippet confirmed verbatim at DiscordWebhook.java:37 (private static final DecimalFormat MONEY = new DecimalFormat("#,##0.00")). The concurrency chain also confirmed: WebhookDispatcherService.init() (line 119) builds a fixed thread pool of deliveryConcurrency threads; deliverDue() invokeAll's the batch across it (line 200); each deliver() calls DiscordWebhook.toPayload(d) (line 230), which reaches money() -> MONEY.format() (DiscordWebhook.java:84). java.text.DecimalFormat is documented as not synchronized, so concurrent format() calls on the one shared static instance can interleave internal DigitList state (garbled digits) or throw AIOOBE. Defect is real and easily triggered whenever a batch contains 2+ Discord deliveries. However major overstates the impact: the corrupted value lives only in a transient Discord embed notification (never persisted to the ledger, which is written solely by the DB trigger), and deliver() is explicitly designed never to throw out (invokeAll just waits; a format() exception fails only that one delivery, not the batch/loop per the ADT-116/117 comments at lines 188-223). Blast radius is a cosmetic notification for one delivery in a game economy, not money movement or data integrity. Fair severity is minor.

description: The integration-test schema is a hand-maintained DDL stand-in duplicated from economy-flyway rather than applying the authoritative Flyway migrations, and it deliberately omits the trg_postings_ai balance-maintenance trigger (comment lines 137-139). This is real MariaDB (good — no H2 dialect risk), but the stand-in can silently drift from the production schema: a column type/width/nullability or new NOT NULL added in a future V<n> migration won't be reflected here, so mapper tests can pass against a schema production no longer matches. Because the trigger is absent, no test verifies that postings actually update account_balances_mat — the balance-write contract that is the ledger's core invariant is unverified end-to-end.
recommendation: Prefer running the economy-flyway migrations against the embedded MariaDB (Flyway can execute DELIMITER-bearing migrations) so the test schema is the authoritative one, or add a CI check that diffs the stand-in DDL against the latest migration. At minimum, add one test that installs the trigger and asserts a transfer moves the materialised balance.
